content:
6 min read
1 hour ago

--

If you’ve ever built a system where one service needs to tell many other services that something happened — a new order, a signup, a payment, a location update — you’ve run into the exact problem Apache Kafka was built to solve.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This article walks through:

• What Kafka actually is (in plain English)

• Why you need it — with a real before/after example

• How to install it (Docker, and a manual setup)

• How to create topics, produce, and consume messages

• A working Node.js/TypeScript example

• Common mistakes to avoid

1. What Is Kafka, Really?
Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform. Strip away the buzzwords, and it’s really just a very fast, very durable, highly scalable log.
Services write events (“messages”) into Kafka. Other services read those events, independently and at their own pace. Kafka doesn’t care who’s listening — it just keeps the log.
┌─────────────┐
Producer ──▶ │ │ ──▶ Consumer A (Email service)
(Order API) │ KAFKA │ ──▶ Consumer B (Analytics)
Producer ──▶ │ TOPIC │ ──▶ Consumer C (Inventory)
(Payments) │ │
└─────────────┘The core building blocks
Concept What it means Broker A single Kafka server. A cluster is made up of multiple brokers. Topic A named stream of events, e.g. orders.created. Partition A topic is split into partitions for parallelism and ordering guarantees. Producer An app that writes/publishes messages to a topic. Consumer An app that reads/subscribes to messages from a topic. Consumer Group A set of consumers sharing the work of reading a topic. Offset A pointer marking how far a consumer has read in a partition.
(Suggested image: a diagram of a topic split into 3 partitions, each holding an ordered log of messages with offsets.)

2. Why Do You Actually Need Kafka?
Here’s the problem Kafka solves, illustrated with a common scenario.
The “Before” — tightly coupled services
Imagine an e-commerce Order Service. When an order is placed, it needs to:

• Send a confirmation email

• Update inventory

• Notify the shipping service

• Log the event for analytics
A naive approach calls each service directly:
Order Service → calls Email Service (HTTP)
→ calls Inventory Service (HTTP)
→ calls Shipping Service (HTTP)
→ calls Analytics Service (HTTP)Problems with this:

• If Email Service is down, does the whole order fail?

• Adding a new consumer (say, a fraud-detection service) means modifying Order Service’s code.

• If Inventory Service is slow, the customer’s checkout request hangs.

• No replay: if Analytics Service was down for an hour, that data is gone.
The “After” — Kafka as the buffer
Order Service → publishes "order.created" event → KafkaKafka topic: order.created
├── Email Service (consumes independently)
├── Inventory Service (consumes independently)
├── Shipping Service (consumes independently)
└── Analytics Service (consumes independently, even if it's down for a while)Order Service now does one thing: publish an event. It doesn’t know or care who’s listening. New consumers can be added later with zero changes to Order Service. If a consumer goes down, it just resumes from its last offset when it comes back — no data lost.
When you actually need it
Kafka earns its complexity when you have:

• Multiple services that need the same event (fan-out)

• High-throughput data (clickstreams, IoT sensors, logs, metrics)

• A need to replay historical events (rebuild a cache, backfill a new service)

• Strict ordering requirements per key (e.g., all events for user_id=123 processed in order)
If you just need one service to call another and get an immediate response, Kafka is overkill — a simple REST call or a lightweight queue (like SQS or RabbitMQ) is usually the better fit.

3. Installing Kafka
There are two common paths. Docker is the fastest way to get a working setup for local development.
Option A: Docker Compose (recommended for local dev)
Create a file named docker-compose.yml:
version: "3.8"
services:
kafka:
image: apache/kafka:latest
container_name: kafka
ports:
- "9092:9092"
environment:
KAFKA_NODE_ID: 1
KAFKA_PROCESS_ROLES: broker,controller
KAFKA_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://:9092,CONTROLLER://:9093
KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9092
KAFKA_CONTROLLER_LISTENER_NAMES: CONTROLLER
KAFKA_CONTROLLER_QUORUM_VOTERS: 1@kafka:9093
KAFKA_LISTENER_SECURITY_PROTOCOL_MAP: CONTROLLER:PLAINTEXT,PLAINTEXT:PLAINTEXT
KAFKA_OFFSETS_TOPIC_REPLICATION_FACTOR: 1Then start it:
docker compose up -dThat’s it — no separate ZooKeeper needed. Modern Kafka (KRaft mode) runs without ZooKeeper entirely.
Option B: Manual installation (Linux/macOS)
# 1. Make sure Java 17+ is installed
java -version# 2. Download the latest Kafka binary
curl -O https://downloads.apache.org/kafka/3.8.0/kafka_2.13-3.8.0.tgz
tar -xzf kafka_2.13-3.8.0.tgz
cd kafka_2.13-3.8.0
# 3. Generate a cluster ID and format storage (KRaft mode, no ZooKeeper)
KAFKA_CLUSTER_ID="$(bin/kafka-storage.sh random-uuid)"
bin/kafka-storage.sh format -t $KAFKA_CLUSTER_ID -c config/kraft/server.properties
# 4. Start the broker
bin/kafka-server-start.sh config/kraft/server.properties
Check the official Kafka downloads page for the current stable version number before running these commands, since version numbers change over time.
(Suggested image: a screenshot of your terminal after step 4, showing the broker successfully started.)
Verifying it works
# Create a topic
bin/kafka-topics.sh --create --topic test-topic \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
--partitions 3 --replication-factor 1# List topics to confirm
bin/kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server localhost:9092If test-topic shows up, Kafka is running correctly.

4. Producing and Consuming from the Command Line
Open two terminal windows.
Terminal 1 — start a consumer (it will wait for messages):
bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh --topic test-topic \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092Terminal 2 — start a producer and type messages:
bin/kafka-console-producer.sh --topic test-topic \
--bootstrap-server localhost:9092Type a message and hit enter. It will instantly appear in Terminal 1. This is Kafka in its simplest form — a durable pipe between a writer and a reader.

5. A Real Example: Node.js/TypeScript Producer and Consumer
Most developers building on Next.js or Node.js backends will use KafkaJS.
npm install kafkajsProducer — publishing an “order created” event
// producer.ts
import { Kafka } from "kafkajs";const kafka = new Kafka({
clientId: "order-service",
brokers: ["localhost:9092"],
});
const producer = kafka.producer();
async function publishOrderCreated(orderId: string, userId: string) {
await producer.connect();
await producer.send({
topic: "order.created",
messages: [
{
key: userId, // ensures all events for this user go to the same partition
value: JSON.stringify({
orderId,
userId,
createdAt: new Date().toISOString(),
}),
},
],
});
await producer.disconnect();
}
publishOrderCreated("order_123", "user_456");
Consumer — an email service reacting to the event
// consumer.ts
import { Kafka } from "kafkajs";const kafka = new Kafka({
clientId: "email-service",
brokers: ["localhost:9092"],
});
const consumer = kafka.consumer({ groupId: "email-service-group" });
async function run() {
await consumer.connect();
await consumer.subscribe({ topic: "order.created", fromBeginning: true });
await consumer.run({
eachMessage: async ({ message }) => {
const event = JSON.parse(message.value?.toString() ?? "{}");
console.log(`Sending confirmation email for order ${event.orderId}`);
// sendEmail(event.userId, event.orderId) ...
},
});
}
run().catch(console.error);Run the consumer first, then the producer. You’ll see the consumer react to the event the moment it’s published — and if you add a second consumer group (say, inventory-service-group), it will get the same event independently, without touching the producer's code at all.
(Suggested image: two boxes — “email-service-group” and “inventory-service-group” — both reading from the same “order.created” topic, each with their own offset.)

6. Common Mistakes to Avoid

• Using one giant topic for everything. Design topics around business events (order.created, payment.failed), not around technical convenience.

• Not setting a message key. Without a key, Kafka spreads related messages across partitions randomly, which breaks per-entity ordering (e.g., events for the same user arriving out of order).

• Treating Kafka like a database. Kafka retains data for a configured period (or size) — it’s a durable log with retention, not permanent storage. Use retention policies deliberately.

• Ignoring consumer group IDs. Two consumers with the same groupId split the work between them (each message goes to only one). Two consumers with different group IDs each get their own full copy of the stream. Mixing this up is one of the most common production bugs.

• Skipping partition planning. The number of partitions limits how many consumers in a group can process a topic in parallel. Too few partitions and you can’t scale consumers; too many creates overhead you may not need.

• Not monitoring consumer lag. If consumers fall behind the rate of production, lag builds up silently until something breaks downstream. Track it from day one.

Wrapping Up
Kafka isn’t just “a message queue” — it’s a shift in how services communicate: from direct, tightly-coupled calls to independent, replayable event streams. Once one team adopts it for a single use case, it tends to become the backbone other teams build on too.
If you’re building anything with multiple services that need to react to the same events — orders, signups, payments, sensor data — it’s worth the setup cost.
Have you used Kafka in production? What tripped you up the first time? Drop a comment below — I’d love to compare notes.

If this was useful, follow for more deep dives into backend architecture, Next.js, and system design.
